title: The Backstories Pointless Trivia or Valid Excuses
display_title: The Backstories Pointless Trivia or Valid Excuses
wikitext:
[]: The Backstories-Pointless Trivia or Valid Excuses?             Quote Post by CyberXIII » Sun Apr 29, 2012 7:12 pm (Warning: Wall of Text) I've been running into a few people that have often complained about the abundance of characters whose motivations are basically "my childhood sucked so I want to end the world!". There's various permutations of this (rape's become more common as merely being an orphan ain't edgy or grimdark enough). With that out of the way, I wonder how this applies to NGE? Do the characters various crappy lives excuse what they do? Does Gendo's dead wife and various psychoses excuse his attempt to end the world? Does Ritsuko's mother issue excuse killing Rei and attempting to blow up NERV Japan? This applies to the protagonists (there are no heroes in NGE, only protagonists and villains) as well. Does Shinji's mental breakdown excuse his attempt at ending the world? Does Asuka's own issues excuse her being a utterly horrid person right up until the day she died? Do Misato's daddy issues excuse her sending a group of child soldiers out to probably die? Or should we ignore their backstories and treat them as we would real people? Let's face it, when somebody punches you in the face, you don't care what happened to him or who he might have lost. You just care that he punched you. Same principle should apply in fiction, but it doesn't. People (particularly fangirls) eat up these backstories, and why? Well, as Boa Hancock put it: "No matter what I may do... whether I kick a kitten, tear off your ears, even slaughter innocent people... the world will never cease to forgive my actions. Why, you ask? Why, it is because I am... beautiful!!!" What comes next is something along the lines of "I can change them!" So, I turn to this corner of the fanbase. Do the characters' own problems excuse what they do, or should we ignore their backstories and condemn their actions? Or is there a third option I'm ignoring? (Probably) Thoughts?

[]:             Quote Post by Henry Spencer » Sun Apr 29, 2012 7:25 pm In Ritsuko's case, those acts are justified in that they were attempts to deter the Third Impact entirely. Killing the Rei reserves allowed Rei III to be free of Gendo's machinations. Furthermore, imploding Terminal Dogma would have destroyed both Lilith and the possibility of the Third Impact occurring entirely. And if you think that your post is a wall of text, you clearly haven't been here long enough...

[]:             Quote Post by A.T. Fish » Sun Apr 29, 2012 7:40 pm It's not as simple as "their backstories shouldn't be of concern, what they are doing is wrong", it's more like "they all have issues, most of them suffer from some degree of depression, and, most importantly, they are facing/have faced an apocalyptic menace". I mean, sociopaths and f****d up people exist even in "normal" conditions, so it's only natural to assume such a dreadful scenario as the one presented to us in NGE would breed some twisted minds.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Sun Apr 29, 2012 8:05 pm The past provides cause, not excuse. Excuses can only be found in the present, just as reasons can only be found in the hoped-for future.

[]:             Quote Post by MightyWings » Sun Apr 29, 2012 8:27 pm The characters are flawed. Real people can be flawed too. I'm sure that there are people in real life that lose spouses, have bad lives, et. that would make others suffer or even end the world for vengeance, selfishness, or whatever other reasons. Unlike Gendo, they don't have the power to destroy the world, and usually end up drudging along through life, or at worst ending their own lives or hurting a comparatively small number of people.

[]:             Quote Post by Stryker » Sun Apr 29, 2012 9:08 pm Here's the thing: Humanity is consisted of irrational, pitiful forms of life. We shall emphasize on "irrational", for this argument. The fact is, if you punched someone in the face because what something that happened to you in the past, or because you lost something, sooner or later, you'll come to realize that you can't justify it, therefore deciding that your decision to punch that person was rash. This is due to humanities impulsive nature. We act before we think. Now, let's put this "idea" (as we will call it) into story making. What makes characters very relational is that we can connect to the characters personality, and in this case, their actions. If your characters thought before they acted, either: 1. They are really smart (if so, it needs to maintain consistency through the story). 2. They are unrealistic (more likely of the two). So, the reason why the characters are written to act before they think is so they are more relational. If a character starts beating the crap out of another because the other did something evil, we would probably be cheering them. But, when later you realize that the character could actually be more useful, you will probably contemplate and say the same thing the character is saying, "Damn that wasn't a good idea." With this relationship and set of actions, you find yourself gradually growing a relationship with the characters (whether positive in the sense that you would've done the same thing, or negative in the sense that you criticize him for making a silly mistake). Although, not extremely, every effort point counts. TL;DR: No, we should condemn him for his actions, because his past can't justify what he does. But, we should at the same time empathize with him, because of the reality that if put into the same situation, we would probably act similarly. To add, it adds to storyline and plot, and therefore never hurts. Never. Nope. Not even once.

[]:             Quote Post by CyberXIII » Sun Apr 29, 2012 9:31 pm I said that was a wall of text because it looked bigger before I posted it. But I digress... First, Ritsuko really didn't care about stopping Third Impact in terms of saving anyone. She wanted petty revenge on Gendo and that just had a lovely side effect of prolonging everyone's lives. Second, and here's the other thing: do you think the characters are better for having these traumatic origin stories or do you think they could have toned it down a bit? Shinji gets this the worst because he's the viewpoint character, but a common complaint about NGE in general (aside from the mindfuck one) is that the characters are all hopeless annoying angst buckets that do nothing but whine. I think that's a pile of shit, but what do you guys and girls think?

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Mon Apr 30, 2012 12:55 am SEELE were the ones with the plan to end the world. Yui first, and subsequently Gendo, had their own agendas that intended to harness the process to try and salvage something better from the process. SEELE also broke Ritsuko and unleashed the inner green-eyed monster that had been simmering just under the surface anyway. Shinji was a teen having the dummy spit to end all dummy spits; and Asuka was, on balance, not a horrid person.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Mon Apr 30, 2012 12:15 pm Mr. Tines wrote: SEELE were the ones with the plan to end the world. Yui first, and subsequently Gendo, had their own agendas that intended to harness the process to try and salvage something better from the process. SEELE also broke Ritsuko and unleashed the inner green-eyed monster that had been simmering just under the surface anyway. Shinji was a teen having the dummy spit to end all dummy spits; and Asuka was, on balance, not a horrid person.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Tue May 01, 2012 9:54 pm Bagheera wrote: Best to place the blame where it really belongs, eh?

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Wed May 02, 2012 5:51 am Reichu wrote: Well, Adam's children are innocents, and Adam probably got fucked over harder than any other single person in the whole mythos, so if we're going to dump blame on anyone let's dump it on Seele for being a bunch of elitist d-bags who think what they want is what's best for everybody.

[]:             Quote Post by A.T. Fish » Wed May 02, 2012 7:58 am The common viewer of NGE is more likely to adopt the anthropocentric view of the show rather than an angelcentric one and thus blame the Angels for all the angst and destruction. Of course there are probably some PETA (People for the Ethical Treatment of Angels) radicals among us to.

[]:             Quote Post by Cody MacArthur Fett » Wed May 02, 2012 2:02 pm Stryker wrote: TL;DR: No, we should condemn him for his actions, because his past can't justify what he does. But, we should at the same time empathize with him, because of the reality that if put into the same situation, we would probably act similarly. To add, it adds to storyline and plot, and therefore never hurts. Never. Nope. Not even once.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Wed May 02, 2012 6:57 pm It's not that they did shit and then got the backstories shoehorned in to justify them, it's that shit happened to them which impaired their abilities to deal with further shit. Of course they could have done better, that's sort of the point of the show, but calling them a bunch of assholes with a little justification as decoration just isn't fair, not after all the detail that was put into these characters, how each of the big revelation was foreshadowed and how we're given all those inner monologues to outline how their problems spring directly from what happened to them - They're only reacting like realistic, imperfect people, and we're shown why. You're oversimplyfing them all, most of all Shinji. Only very few people would give a crap about the fate of humanity after their best friend got maimed, their already barely existant Relationship withv their father went down the drain for good, their mentot figure died, their only parental support of sorts fell into depression, their love interest got raped in all but in name, their pther love interest got blown to smithereens only to be replaced by some scary knockoff, not to mention getting some lovecraftian truths dumped on their skulls and having to kill the first person to tell them they loved them, and those few would be particularly admirable people. Yeah, you should still care about it, but you can't blame an understandably still immsture teenager (who had a sucky childhood and got put in a war situation at a young age, like many a shonen protagonist) for sctong like a teenager - which is why you shouldn't stuff those into doomsday machines, but they had no choice. The villains of the story are SEELE, as many here pointed out, (even if we do have varying shades of grey in this show.) This is why we don't see much of them, because plain evilly evil villains are boring. Also, t's not really that much about justification, even if you had a character who's an absolute monster, if you don't explain WHY that person is a monster, you fail at being a writer and should go make silly cartoons with their generic doomsday villains. They're given backstories to add depth to them and make them more interesting, to make the audience think, no primarily to make the audience like them.

[]: Re: The Backstories-Pointless Trivia or Valid Excuses?             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Wed May 02, 2012 8:06 pm CyberXIII wrote: (there are no heroes in NGE, only protagonists and villains)

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Wed May 02, 2012 10:20 pm Bagheera wrote: Meh. They're innocent until they start blowing shit up and prying into people's minds to say hello.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Thu May 03, 2012 9:55 am Reichu wrote: You probably know that things aren't this simple...

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Thu May 03, 2012 10:49 am A.T. Fish wrote: The common viewer of NGE is more likely to adopt the anthropocentric view of the show rather than an angelcentric one and thus blame the Angels for all the angst and destruction.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Thu May 03, 2012 11:24 am Reichu wrote: This is "innocent" in the "innocent (as/like children)", i.e. of consequences, sense, not the "guilty vs. innocent" sense. Adam's children aren't socially conditioned; they'll do things that are inappropriate because nobody ever explained why they shouldn't.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Thu May 03, 2012 12:38 pm Bagheera wrote: That amounts to whitewashing IMO.
